text: We present the case of a sixty nine year old man treated with TATME after stenting of an obstructed rectal cancer. In January, two thousand seventeen, this gentleman presented as an emergency to the hospital whilst on holiday in Sydney, Australia, and was found to have an obstructed cancer of the rectal sigmoid. An endoluminal stent was positioned to resolve the obstruction. Once back in the UK, he was investigated with a PET and an MRI, which demonstrated a T-IV-N-II rectal cancer, approximately nine centimetres from the anal verge, with two potentially resectable liver metastases. After four cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy and restaging scans, he underwent removal of his tempted primary rectal cancer.

The surgeon begins the operation by mobilizing the splenic flexure, followed by the division of the inferior mesenteric vein and artery. The total mesorectal excision (TME) plane is entered using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, navigating challenges due to inflammation from the stent. Anterolateral dissection is performed to include the peritoneal reflection, which appears involved by disease, and the anterior dissection progresses to the level of the seminal vesicles.

The surgeon clamps the colon at the sigmoid level using a shohan. The stent is clearly visible, and a purse string suture is placed distal to it. The surgical field is thoroughly washed with iodine solution before and after positioning the suture. Circumferential marks are made with a diatomite hook, and dissection begins.

The surgeon performs circumferential dissection around the rectum, encountering difficulty in identifying surgical planes due to inflammation from the stent. The lateral plane is easier to dissect, allowing for circumferential leveling. The dissection is completed both posteriorly and anteriorly.

A window is created on the right side of the rectum, allowing access to the peritoneal cavity and connection with the abdominal dissection. The bulky rectum, due to the stent, is extracted through an extended umbilical incision using a wound protector.

The surgeon performs an end-to-end anastomosis using a III-III EEA stapler, followed by the creation of a defunctional loop ileostomy in the right iliac fossa. The TME dissection is visually confirmed to be within the mesorectal plane. The excised specimen shows a clear circumferential resection margin.

The patient didn't have any major complications and underwent liver metastasectomy in February, he's now waiting for the loop ileostomy to be closed. In conclusion, we think that the transrenal approach should be gold standard for resecting a stented rectal cancer to avoid the use of a linear stapler to transect the lower rectum, allowing a safe and precise DRM and avoiding the risk of cutting through the stent or fracturing the tumour.
